references.title: Architectural Adaptation and Regularization of Attention Networks for Incremental Knowledge Tracing

extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
Attention-based KT models are the curent state-of-the-art on multiple datasets. However, most of them assume that the pool of questions does not change after the training of the model. 

Adding questions in online settings would lead to an increase of the input dimension.

Moreover, the changes in behavior of the learners introduce distributional drift.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
The proposed model uses an attention mechanism for questions and concepts that increases dynamically while preserving the information provided by the pre-existing pool of questions. 

In addition, the model weights are regularized.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
This model is based on AKT with a change in the question-concept mapping. 

This approach involves the ability to increase the dimensionality of the attention module whether it is because of the introduction of new concepts or the introduction of new questions.

This is done by copying the weights of the previous question while initializing the new weights for the new question using Rasch embedding.
extracted.Models: J-AKT, D-AKT, ARAIKT, ARAIKT without reg
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a change to the attention representation of AKT and show that it outperforms AKT on the tested dataset. 

Authors also perform an ablation study to showcase the improtance of having regularization or not.

annotations.text: A limitation of the current embedding based representations of KT models is the fact that the number of questions is often fixed. Thus, when EdTech platforms add new questions, the current models cannot follow this update.

Authors propose a model that adapts to growing pools of concepts and/or questions. The model, called ARAIKT rpeserves previous representations while being able to adapt to increasing concepts and question banks.
Testing of the model on portions of the ASSISTment dataset showcase effectiveness of the approach.
